1.text: Physical activity has been consistently shown to confer benefits for various health outcomes in adults, including improved all-cause mortality, cardiovascular disease mortality, incident hypertension, incident site-specific cancers (such as bladder, breast, colon, endometrial, oesophageal adenocarcinoma, gastric, and renal), 1 incident type-2 diabetes, mental health (reduced symptoms of anxiety and depression), cognitive health, and sleep. The World Health Organization's guidelines on physical activity and sedentary behaviour emphasize the importance of regular physical activity in reducing these risks. In older adults, physical activity is particularly beneficial for overall treatment plan and can help mitigate poor health outcomes associated with higher amounts of sedentary behaviour, including all-cause mortality, cardiovascular disease mortality, and cancer mortality. Pre-exercise medical clearance is generally unnecessary for individuals without contraindications prior to beginning light- or moderate-intensity physical activity not exceeding the demands of brisk walking or everyday living.

Adults and older adults aged 18 years and older with chronic conditions such as cancer, hypertension, type-2 diabetes, or HIV can benefit from regular physical activity. Strong evidence supports the recommendation that these individuals engage in muscle-strengthening activities at moderate to high intensity involving all major muscle groups on at least two days a week. This provides additional health benefits beyond those already associated with regular physical activity for adults and older adults without chronic conditions. The benefits include improved all-cause mortality, cancer-specific mortality, cardiovascular disease mortality, and reduced rates of mortality from cardiovascular disease and indicators of disease progression. Physical activity also improves physical function, health-related quality of life, and mental health outcomes such as reduced symptoms of anxiety and depression in people living with HIV. When unable to meet the recommended intensity or frequency, individuals should aim to engage in physical activity according to their abilities, starting with small amounts and gradually increasing over time. Pre-exercise medical clearance is generally not necessary for individuals without contraindications prior to beginning light- or moderate-intensity physical activity.

Physical activity encompasses any bodily movement produced by skeletal muscles requiring energy expenditure, including occupational physical activity, sport, and transport domain activities such as walking or cycling. Sedentary behaviour is characterized by an energy expenditure of 1.5 METS or lower while sitting, reclining, or lying, encompassing desk-based office work, driving a car, and watching television. The guidelines operationalize sedentary behaviour to include self-reported low movement sitting, leisure time, occupational, and total screen time, as well as low levels of movement measured by devices assessing posture. Physical inactivity is defined as an insufficient physical activity level to meet present recommendations. Sedentary screen time excludes active screen-based games requiring physical activity or movement. The guidelines aim to reduce sedentary behaviour, particularly recreational screen time, in children and adolescents, with evidence suggesting higher amounts of sedentary behaviour are associated with poor health outcomes such as increased adiposity, poorer cardiometabolic health, and reduced sleep duration.

This document outlines the terms and conditions for using World Health Organization (WHO) publications, including guidelines on physical activity and sedentary behaviour. The work is to be cited appropriately, with no suggestion of endorsement by WHO, and must be licensed under the same or equivalent Creative Commons licence if adapted. Translations should include a disclaimer stating that WHO is not responsible for the content. Cataloguing-in-Publication (CIP) data are available online, while sales, rights, and licensing information can be found on the WHO website. Permission is required to reuse third-party materials, with the risk of claims from infringement resting solely with the user. General disclaimers apply, including those regarding the legal status of countries and territories, and the mention of proprietary products does not imply endorsement or recommendation by WHO. The document also notes that reasonable precautions have been taken to verify information, but liability for damages arising from its use is not assumed.

The World Health Organization (WHO) Guidelines on Physical Activity and Sedentary Behaviour provide recommendations for adults aged 18-64 years to reduce the risk of chronic diseases. The guidelines emphasize the importance of regular physical activity, aiming for at least 150 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic physical activity or 75 minutes of vigorous-intensity aerobic physical activity per week. Additionally, adults should engage in muscle-strengthening activities on two or more days a week. Sedentary behaviour is also discouraged, with recommendations to limit recreational screen time to less than 2 hours per day and reduce occupational sitting time. The guidelines acknowledge that individual circumstances may vary, such as age, sex, and health status, and provide tailored advice for different populations. For example, older adults are advised to engage in more frequent physical activity, while pregnant women should avoid high-impact activities. The WHO also emphasizes the need for a comprehensive approach to promoting physical activity and reducing sedentary behaviour, involving governments, healthcare systems, and communities.

Aerobic physical activity, characterized by sustained rhythmic movement of large muscles, improves cardiorespiratory fitness and is contrasted with anaerobic physical activity, which involves brief intense bursts of exercise such as weightlifting and sprints. Bone-strengthening activities, including running, jumping rope, and lifting weights, are designed to promote bone growth and strength through impact or tension forces on specific sites in the skeletal system. Cardiometabolic health encompasses the interplay of blood pressure, blood lipids, blood glucose, and insulin on overall health.

Physical activity levels can be assessed across various domains, including leisure-time, occupation, education, household, and transportation. Exercise is a planned, structured, repetitive, and purposeful physical activity aimed at improving or maintaining physical fitness, performance, or health. Executive function encompasses constructs such as working memory, cognitive flexibility, and inhibitory control. Fitness measures the body's ability to function efficiently in work and leisure activities, including physical fitness and cardiorespiratory fitness.

Flexibility is a health- and performance-related component of physical fitness that refers to the range of motion possible at a joint, influenced by variables such as ligament and tendon tightness. Functional exercises, like tandem stands and chair squats, can be embedded into everyday tasks to improve lower-body strength, balance, and motor performance. Household domain physical activity involves domestic duties, while leisure-domain physical activity is performed for enjoyment or recreation, not essential to daily living. Disability is defined as the negative aspects of interaction between an individual with a health condition an

Recommendations for adults aged 18-64 years and children/adolescents aged 5-17 years are as follows. Adults should limit sedentary time, replacing it with physical activity of any intensity, to provide health benefits. Strong evidence supports regular physical activity, with at least 150-300 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic activity or 75-150 minutes of vigorous-intensity aerobic activity per week for substantial health benefits. Incorporating vigorous-intensity aerobic activities and strengthening muscle and bone exercises on at least three days a week is also recommended. Adults should aim to do more than the recommended levels of physical activity, particularly in reducing detrimental effects of high sedentary behavior. Pregnant and postpartum women, as well as those with chronic medical conditions or disability, are advised to meet these recommendations where possible. The guidelines emphasize that most physical activity should be aerobic, with moderate- to vigorous-intensity activities being the focus.

The World Health Organization (WHO) published guidelines on physical activity and sedentary behaviour in 2020, available in electronic and print formats. The document is licensed under the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 3.0 IGO licence, allowing non-commercial use and adaptation with proper citation. The WHO logo cannot be used, and any translation must be licensed under the same or equivalent terms. The guidelines were developed by an external review group, including experts such as Kingsley Akinroye and Emmanuel Stamatakis, in collaboration with WHO staff. The document includes a disclaimer stating that WHO is not responsible for the content or accuracy of translations, and that readers are solely responsible for interpreting and using the material. The publication also includes information on cataloguing-in-publication data, sales rights, and licensing, as well as general disclaimers regarding liability and warranty.

Physical exercise, particularly cord injury rehabilitation, has been shown to improve various aspects of health and performance in individuals with cognitive impairments. Executive function constructs such as working memory, cognitive flexibility, and inhibitory control are enhanced through physical activity, leading to improved physical function and cognition in individuals with Parkinson's disease and those with a history of stroke. Additionally, cord injury rehabilitation has been found to improve walking function, muscular strength, and upper extremity function, resulting in enhanced health-related quality of life for individuals with diseases or disorders that impair cognitive function. Furthermore, physical exercise has been linked to beneficial effects on cognition and may improve quality of life in adults with schizophrenia, intellectual disability, and major clinical depression. Notably, excessive sedentary behavior is associated with poor outcomes, including reduced physical fitness and cardiorespiratory fitness. Overall, the benefits of physical exercise on cognitive function and overall health are significant, particularly for individuals with impairments that affect executive function and motor abilities.

A cluster of reviews on WHO guidelines for physical activity and sedentary behavior were conducted by multiple research teams from Australia, South Africa, and Europe. The reviews, led by Carrie Patnode and Michelle Henninger, examined the evidence for physical activity recommendations in children and adolescents aged 5-17 years. The GRADE tables assessed the quality of the evidence, which included systematic reviews and meta-analyses on aerobic exercise, anaerobic exercise, balance training, and bone-strengthening activities. The reviews concluded that moderate-to-vigorous intensity aerobic physical activity should be done for at least 60 minutes per day across the week, with strong recommendations based on moderate certainty evidence. The WHO guidelines emphasize the importance of a balanced approach to physical activity, including both aerobic exercise and anaerobic exercise, as well as balance training and bone-strengthening activities. The reviews also highlighted the need for consideration of individual factors, such as age, sex, and body mass index (BMI), when developing physical activity recommendations.

Pregnant and postpartum women should undertake regular physical activity throughout pregnancy and postpartum to confer benefits on maternal and fetal health, including decreased risk of pre-eclampsia, gestational hypertension, and postpartum depression. The American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists recommends at least 150 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic physical activity per week for substantial health benefits. Women who habitually engaged in vigorous-intensity aerobic activity before pregnancy can continue these activities during pregnancy and the postpartum period. Physical activity should be incorporated into a variety of aerobic and muscle-strengthening activities, with gentle stretching also being beneficial. Pregnant and postpartum women are advised to start with small amounts of physical activity and gradually increase frequency, intensity, and duration over time. They should avoid physical activity during excessive heat, stay hydrated, and participate in low-risk activities. The guidelines emphasize that doing some physical activity is better than none, even if the recommended amount is not met, and that pelvic floor muscle training can help reduce urinary incontinence risk.

Physical activity during pregnancy and postpartum has been extensively studied, with numerous benefits observed for maternal and fetal health. Regular physical activity throughout pregnancy and postpartum is recommended for all pregnant and postpartum women without contraindication, as it decreases the risk of pre-eclampsia, gestational hypertension, gestational diabetes, excessive gestational weight gain, delivery complications, and postpartum depression, while also reducing newborn complications and birthweight. Notably, physical activity has no adverse effects on birthweight and does not increase the risk of stillbirth. The 2019 Canadian guideline for physical activity during pregnancy was developed based on a comprehensive review of 12 systematic reviews, which assessed over 25,000 studies in English, Spanish, and French language. These reviews informed the development of WHO recommendations, with updated evidence profiles presented in the Web Annex: Evidence profiles.

2.text:
The World Health Organization's guidelines on physical activity and sedentary behavior recommend reducing chronic diseases by promoting regular physical activity and limiting sedentary behavior among adults aged 18-64 years and children/adolescents aged 5-17 years. Adults should engage in at least 150 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic physical activity or 75 minutes of vigorous-intensity aerobic physical activity per week, with muscle-strengthening activities on two or more days a week. Sedentary behavior is discouraged, with limits set for recreational screen time and occupational sitting time. Pregnant women are advised to avoid high-impact activities, while older adults should engage in more frequent physical activity. The guidelines emphasize a comprehensive approach involving governments, healthcare systems, and communities.

Moderate-to-vigorous intensity aerobic physical activity is recommended for at least 60 minutes per day across the week, with strong evidence supporting this recommendation. Anaerobic exercise, balance training, and bone-strengthening activities are also emphasized as part of a balanced approach to physical activity. Individual factors such as age, sex, and body mass index (BMI) should be considered when developing physical activity recommendations. The guidelines aim to reduce sedentary behavior, particularly recreational screen time, in children and adolescents, with evidence suggesting higher amounts of sedentary behavior are associated with poor health outcomes.

The document also includes general disclaimers regarding legal status of countries and territories, proprietary products, and warranty. Developed in collaboration with external experts such as Kingsley Akinroye and Emmanuel Stamatakis, WHO guidelines were published in 2020 in electronic and print formats under the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 3.0 IGO licence.

Physical activity is consistently associated with improved health outcomes across various populations, including adults and older adults with chronic conditions such as cancer, hypertension, type-2 diabetes, and HIV. The World Health Organization's guidelines emphasize the importance of regular physical activity in reducing risks of all-cause mortality, cardiovascular disease mortality, and cancer mortality. For individuals aged 18 years and older with chronic conditions, muscle-strengthening activities at moderate to high intensity involving all major muscle groups are recommended on at least two days a week. This regimen provides additional health benefits beyond regular physical activity for adults and older adults without chronic conditions, including improved cardiorespiratory fitness, bone density, and mental health outcomes.

Physical activity encompasses various domains, including aerobic exercise, anaerobic exercise, bone-strengthening activities, and flexibility exercises. Aerobic exercise improves cardiorespiratory fitness, while anaerobic exercise is characterized by brief intense bursts of exercise such as weightlifting and sprints. Bone-strengthening activities promote bone growth and strength through impact or tension forces on specific sites in the skeletal system. Physical activity levels can be assessed across various domains, including leisure-time, occupation, education, household, and transportation.

Regular physical activity has been shown to improve executive function constructs such as working memory, cognitive flexibility, and inhibitory control, leading to enhanced physical function and cognition in individuals with Parkinson's disease and those with a history of stroke.
